options: The period from delivery until 2 weeks postpartum
The time from delivery until 6 weeks (42 days) postpartum
The period of labor and delivery
s2.explain: Puerperium = time from delivery till 6 weeks (42 days) postpartum when pregnancy changes resolve and the body reverts to the non-pregnant state.

s2.q: Which statement about the puerperium is TRUE?
options: All pregnancy changes reverse within 2 weeks
Six weeks is the standard duration of the puerperium
The uterus returns to normal size by 2 weeks
s2.explain: Six weeks is the standard duration. Uterine involution takes the full 6 weeks (1000g at delivery → 60-80g by 6 weeks).

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: Immediately after labor, the uterine fundus is at:
s3.explain: Immediately after labor the fundus is at the umbilicus. It descends ~1-2 cm per day and should not be palpable abdominally by day 10-14.

s3.q: The uterine weight at the end of the puerperium is:
s3.explain: Uterine involution: 1000 g (immediately) → 500 g (1 wk) → 250 g (2 wks) → 60-80 g (6 wks, non-pregnant).

s3.q: In non-lactating women, ovulation and menses typically return:
s3.explain: Non-lactating: menses return at 1.5-2 months. Lactating: 6 months or earlier (LAM criteria apply).

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s3.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s4.q: Which of the following is a normal general body change during the puerperium?
options: Bradycardia (<60 bpm) is common in the first week
s4.explain: Bradycardia (not tachycardia) is normal in early puerperium. Tachycardia >100 bpm suggests infection or anemia.

options: Painful uterine contractions that increase with suckling due to oxytocin release
Always a sign of retained products
s4.explain: After-pains are normal painful contractions, increasing with suckling (oxytocin release). Commoner in multiparas.

s4.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s4.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s4.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s4.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s6.q: How frequently should uterine massage and bleeding monitoring be done in the immediate postpartum period?
options: Every 15 minutes for at least 1 hour
s6.explain: Every 15 min for at least 1 hr — this covers the highest-risk window for primary PPH.

s6.q: Anti-D immunoglobulin should be given to Rh-negative mothers with Rh-positive babies within:
s6.explain: Anti-D must be given within the first 72 hours post-delivery. Ideally dose by Kleihauer-Betke test if fetomaternal hemorrhage is suspected.

s6.q: Which of the following is part of the 11-pillar management of normal puerperium?
options: Semi-sitting position to encourage lochia drainage
NPO for 24 hours after vaginal delivery
Ambulation should be avoided for 3 days
Constipation is encouraged to promote uterine involution
s6.explain: Semi-sitting position encourages drainage of lochia. Diet is allowed 2 hrs after uncomplicated vaginal delivery and ambulation is encouraged to prevent DVT.

s6.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s6.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s6.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s6.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s7.q: The MOST common cause of puerperal pyrexia is:
s7.explain: Milk engorgement (days 3-5) is the most common cause. However, puerperal sepsis is the most serious — 'sepsis until proved otherwise' is the rule.

s7.q: Puerperal pyrexia is defined as temperature of ≥38°C lasting ≥24 hours during the first:
s7.explain: Definition: temperature reaching 38°C or more AND lasting 24 hours or more during the first 10 days of puerperium.

s7.q: Which of the following is NOT a cause of uterine subinvolution?
s7.explain: Subinvolution causes (RIAN): Retained products, Infection, Antepartum overdistension, Non-lactating women. Lactation actually promotes involution.

s7.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s7.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s7.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s7.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
